source_paper_title: Many-Server Asymptotics for Join-the-Shortest-Queue in the Super-Halfin-Whitt Scaling Window
context:
Page 10, Section 2 (Remark 2.9)

Interestingly, indeed, if the load is much heavier, that is, \(\lambda_N = 1 - O(N^{-\alpha})\) with \(\alpha > 2\), then the result in [23] implies that the appropriately scaled total number of tasks under the JSQ policy has the same limiting distribution as the centralized system. It will be an interesting future direction to identify the precise scaling of the system load where this transition of behavior occurs.
title: Identify the load-scaling threshold where JSQ steady-state matches centralized \(M/M/N\) in heavy traffic
background_latex:
Consider a homogeneous system of \(N\) parallel single-server queues under the Join-the-Shortest-Queue (JSQ) routing policy. Jobs arrive as a Poisson process of total rate \(\lambda(N)\) and have i.i.d. exponential service times with mean \(1\). Let the per-server load be \(\lambda_N:=\lambda(N)/N\). Let \(S^{(N)}(\infty)\) denote the total number of jobs in steady state for the \(N\)-server JSQ system.

For comparison, consider the centralized \(M/M/N\) queue (single common waiting line feeding \(N\) exponential servers) with the same arrival rate \(\lambda(N)\); let \(S^{\mathrm{cent},(N)}(\infty)\) be its steady-state total number of jobs.

Assume a many-server heavy-traffic scaling of the form
\[\lambda_N = 1-\frac{\beta}{N^{\alpha}},\]
where \(\beta>0\) is fixed and \(\alpha>0\) is a scaling exponent. (Equivalently, \(\lambda(N)=N-\beta N^{1-\alpha}\).)

Let \(g_{\alpha}(N)\) be a deterministic scaling (depending on \(\alpha\)) and \(c_{\alpha}(N)\) a deterministic centering such that the scaled steady-state total-job count
\[Y^{(N)}_{\alpha}:=\frac{S^{(N)}(\infty)-c_{\alpha}(N)}{g_{\alpha}(N)}\]
has a non-degenerate weak limit as \(N\to\infty\). Similarly define the centralized analogue
\[Y^{\mathrm{cent},(N)}_{\alpha}:=\frac{S^{\mathrm{cent},(N)}(\infty)-c_{\alpha}(N)}{g_{\alpha}(N)}.\]
In the centralized \(M/M/N\) system, for appropriate heavy-traffic choices of \(c_{\alpha}(N)\) and \(g_{\alpha}(N)\), the limiting law is exponential with rate \(\beta\) (equivalently mean \(1/\beta\)) in the regimes referenced by the paper.
problem_definition_latex:
Problem: Identify the precise exponent threshold \(\alpha_c\) (and corresponding centering \(c_{\alpha}(N)\) and scaling \(g_{\alpha}(N)\)) such that, under \(\lambda_N=1-\beta/N^{\alpha}\), the JSQ steady-state total-job count has the same many-server heavy-traffic limit as the centralized \(M/M/N\) queue.

More concretely, determine for which \(\alpha>0\) one can choose \(c_{\alpha}(N),g_{\alpha}(N)\) so that
\[Y^{(N)}_{\alpha}\ \xRightarrow[N\to\infty]{}\ \mathrm{Exp}(\beta),\]
(i.e., convergence in distribution to an exponential random variable with rate \(\beta\)), matching the corresponding limit of \(Y^{\mathrm{cent},(N)}_{\alpha}\); and determine for which \(\alpha\) such matching is impossible (i.e., the JSQ limit law differs from \(\mathrm{Exp}(\beta)\)).

Consider a homogeneous system of \(N\) parallel single-server queues under the Join-the-Shortest-Queue (JSQ) routing policy. Jobs arrive as a Poisson process of total rate \(\lambda(N)\) and have i.i.d. exponential service times with mean \(1\). Let the per-server load be \(\lambda_N:=\lambda(N)/N\). Let \(S^{(N)}(\infty)\) denote the total number of jobs in steady state for the \(N\)-server JSQ system.

For comparison, consider the centralized \(M/M/N\) queue (single common waiting line feeding \(N\) exponential servers) with the same arrival rate \(\lambda(N)\); let \(S^{\mathrm{cent},(N)}(\infty)\) be its steady-state total number of jobs.

Assume a many-server heavy-traffic scaling of the form
\[\lambda_N = 1-\frac{\beta}{N^{\alpha}},\]
where \(\beta>0\) is fixed and \(\alpha>0\) is a scaling exponent. (Equivalently, \(\lambda(N)=N-\beta N^{1-\alpha}\).)

Let \(g_{\alpha}(N)\) be a deterministic scaling (depending on \(\alpha\)) and \(c_{\alpha}(N)\) a deterministic centering such that the scaled steady-state total-job count

Problem:
Problem: Identify the precise exponent threshold \(\alpha_c\) (and corresponding centering \(c_{\alpha}(N)\) and scaling \(g_{\alpha}(N)\)) such that, under \(\lambda_N=1-\beta/N^{\alpha}\), the JSQ steady-state total-job count has the same many-server heavy-traffic limit as the centralized \(M/M/N\) queue.

More concretely, determine for which \(\alpha>0\) one can choose \(c_{\alpha}(N),g_{\alpha}(N)\) so that
\[Y^{(N)}_{\alpha}\ \xRightarrow[N\to\infty]{}\ \mathrm{Exp}(\beta),\]
(i.e., convergence in distribution to an exponential random variable with rate \(\beta\)), matching the corresponding limit of \(Y^{\mathrm{cent},(N)}_{\alpha}\); and determine for which \(\alpha\) such matching is impossible (i.e., the JSQ limit law differs from \(\mathrm{Exp}(\beta)\)).
open_problem_quote: Interestingly, indeed, if the load is much heavier, that is, \(\lambda_N = 1 - O(N^{-\alpha})\) with \(\alpha > 2\), then the result in [23] implies that the appropriately scaled total number of tasks under the JSQ policy has the same limiting distribution as the centralized system. It will be an interesting future direction to identify the precise scaling of the system load where this transition of behavior occurs.
